card_description: Mircea Eliade (1907–1986) was a Romanian historian of religion, philosopher, and professor at the University of Chicago whose work on 'sacred time' and 'mythic return' provided PKD with a vital scholarly framework for decoding his 2-3-74 experience. Dick was particularly influenced by Eliade's concepts of 'illo tempore' (the mythical 'time of the beginning') and the 'eternal return' — the idea that through ritual and myth, human beings can exit 'profane time' and re-enter the sacred, timeless realm of the origins. In the Exegesis, Dick uses Eliade to explain how his perception of first-century Rome superimposed on 1974 California was not a hallucination, but a breakthrough into sacred space-time. Eliade's work on shamanism also informed Dick's understanding of his own role as a visionary who travels between dimensions to recover lost cosmic information. For Dick, Eliade's 'Myth of the Eternal Return' was not just a theory but a description of the ontological mechanism by which the 'Black Iron Prison' is periodically pierced by the 'Palm Tree Garden.' Eliade provided the scholarly vocabulary that allowed Dick to bridge the gap between his personal visionary experiences and the universal structures of human religious history.
full_description: Mircea Eliade (1907–1986) was a Romanian historian of religion, philosopher, and professor at the University of Chicago whose work on 'sacred time' and 'mythic return' provided PKD with a vital scholarly framework for decoding his 2-3-74 experience. Dick was particularly influenced by Eliade's concepts of 'illo tempore' (the mythical 'time of the beginning') and the 'eternal return' — the idea that through ritual and myth, human beings can exit 'profane time' and re-enter the sacred, timeless realm of the origins. In the Exegesis, Dick uses Eliade to explain how his perception of first-century Rome superimposed on 1974 California was not a hallucination, but a breakthrough into sacred space-time. Eliade's work on shamanism also informed Dick's understanding of his own role as a visionary who travels between dimensions to recover lost cosmic information. For Dick, Eliade's 'Myth of the Eternal Return' was not just a theory but a description of the ontological mechanism by which the 'Black Iron Prison' is periodically pierced by the 'Palm Tree Garden.' Eliade provided the scholarly vocabulary that allowed Dick to bridge the gap between his personal visionary experiences and the universal structures of human religious history.

It was done for me, not by me - done for me by the divine messenger / savior from the celestial realm (the Pleroma, where I came from originally); this is he who I saw as Zebra inside + outside me breaking the Karmic (causal) hold over me.
